content:
Hi!

I'm writing a TDP driver for Linux and Windows (as part of a wider project), which will support GPUs (TGP) and CPUs (TDP, Intel/AMD). I've structured the drivers and a 'core' abstraction crate as part of a Rust workspace.

I'm not planning to distribute binaries, but I was curious about the recommended approach to writing a Rust Linux driver in a Rust workspace?

Ideally, I'd like to be able to develop with auto-complete in CLion, but I might have to go with a different approach.

You can find the project here, but I'm about to commit & push some new changes to the core abstractive module: https://github.com/shymega/PwrCtrl.

Thank you! :)

By Rust workspace, do you mean a Cargo one? i.e. you want to have it as a crate together with your other crates and compile it with Cargo?

We support building out-of-tree modules the usual way the kernel supports it, so you could perhaps create a crate in your workspace and use build.rs to invoke the compilation of your out-of-tree module, i.e. calling make. Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/out-of-tree-modules for some pointers, including a basic template of an out-of-tree module.

I hope that helps!

Miguel Ojeda said:

By Rust workspace, do you mean a Cargo one? i.e. you want to have it as a crate together with your other crates and compile it with Cargo?

We support building out-of-tree modules the usual way the kernel supports it, so you could perhaps create a crate in your workspace and use build.rs to invoke the compilation of your out-of-tree module, i.e. calling make. Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/out-of-tree-modules for some pointers, including a basic template of an out-of-tree module.

I hope that helps!

Hey Miguel,

Yeah, I think build.rs is the best option. I believe I've fundamentally misunderstood out-of-tree modules. Perhaps I should reconsider the design, and change it to a user-space daemon, running as root in a restricted sandbox... I'm relying on a few external crates, which I'm not convinced belongs with the kernel.
